narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've already shown remarkable determination in pursuing your dream amid challenges many face, and we appreciate the strengths in your profile like your solid undergraduate GPA of 3.40 and LSAT score of 39, both above average for applicants. Your Year 1 and Year 3 school deciles of 7 also reflect consistent performance in a competitive environment. However, the admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated your risk of failure at 49%, tipping the decision toward rejection to prioritize students with lower predicted risks. The most influential factor pulling against you was your upper-middle family income, which surprisingly nudged the prediction toward a higher chance of bar failure compared to lower-income backgrounds in the dataset. Next, your undergraduate GPA played a notable role in the same direction, as it was seen as not quite strong enough to fully offset other risks despite being above average. On a positive note, your Year 3 decile had the strongest beneficial impact, strongly pushing the model toward predicting bar passage success. Your LSAT score and full-time enrollment status also helped by leaning in favor of passing, though with milder influence. Compared to typical students, where most are full-time like you and averages hover lower on LSAT, GPA, and deciles, your profile is competitive in many ways but held back by how the model weighed income and GPA against passing patterns. This isn't a reflection of your potential as a future lawyer—it's one data-driven snapshot. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT or GPA-boosting post-bac courses to strengthen those areas, seek targeted bar prep resources tailored for underrepresented students like yourself, and apply to programs with holistic reviews that value your unique background. You're capable of rewriting this story—keep pushing, and reach out to admissions counselors for personalized advice. We believe in your journey.
